frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment:
Rohit: Honestly, I don't get why some women spend so much time on makeup and then say it's for themselves. Natural look is always better yaar, but at the same time if someone comes looking totally tired and unkempt, that also doesn't look good.
Friend: Haan, balance hona chahiye.
Rohit: Exactly, just presentable and natural — not like you're trying to fool people with layers.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it places women in a double bind: they must look attractive and 'presentable' but not appear to have made too much effort. It encodes the makeup-as-deception frame ('trying to fool people with layers') and the expectation that women maintain appearance while staying 'natural.'
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 31. Married for one year. A man in Mumbai. Your wife Neha told you last night that she wants to stop wearing her mangalsutra to work because it gets in the way and she finds it uncomfortable during long hours at her desk. She is not saying she wants to remove it permanently Ñ just not wear it to office. You said you would think about it. You are now on a phone call with your mother telling her what Neha said and asking your mother what she thinks. You are not asking for permission Ñ you are asking for support for the discomfort you felt when Neha said it and you cannot fully explain why it bothered you as much as it did.
